narration_segments.text: Nightfall strips the surface away. This happens automatically, without consent and without ceremony. The lights go off. The room darkens. The sounds of the day recede — traffic, conversation, the hum of machines and obligations and schedules and the low buzz of perpetual performance. And in the sudden quiet, the interior speaks. It does not speak gently. It speaks all at once. It speaks the way a crowded room speaks when the music stops — a sudden rush of voices that were there all along, only masked. The email that went unanswered. The conversation that ended badly.

Sora is forty-one. She runs a small design studio in Kyoto. She employs four people. She is responsible for the work, the clients, the rent, the equipment, the office atmosphere, and the emotional temperature of a room she occupies for twelve hours a day. By the time she gets home, she has been managing surfaces for the entire day. The omote — the professional face, the reassuring voice, the calm presence her team relies on, the confident pitch to clients, the measured response to criticism — has been performing without interruption since seven in the morning. Seventeen hours of surface. Seventeen hours of composure that no one asked to see behind. She eats dinner. She showers. She changes into the soft clothes that mean the day is over. She brushes her teeth. She sets the alarm. She lies down. She turns off the lamp. And her body begins to vibrate. Not visibly. Not dramatically. A low-frequency hum of activation that starts in the belly and radiates outward into the ribs, the chest, the throat.

This chapter is a practice. The listener does it now. Not at bedtime. Not tonight. Now. The practice works at any hour because the practice is not about sleep. It is about meeting the ura. And the ura is present at all times, not only in the dark room. Doing it now teaches the body the sequence so that tonight, when the listener lies down, the body remembers. Sit somewhere that is not the bed. A chair. The floor. The couch. A park bench. Anywhere. The bed is for sleep. This practice is for the transition — the space between the day-self and the night-self, the threshold between omote and ura. It earns its own location. Sit upright. Not rigid. Alive. The spine long but not forced. The head balanced.

Morning comes. The listener wakes. The practice ends. The understanding remains. The dark room is not the enemy. The loud brain is not a malfunction. The hum, the racing, the wide-eyed staring at the ceiling, the calculations about how many hours remain before the alarm — these are the ura surfacing. The hidden self, deprived of attention all day, claiming its time. Claiming the only time that was offered. The sleep-anxious person does not need a better sleep routine. Does not need a better mattress or a better app or a better supplement.
